title: Splitting a VC/CV Word into Syllables

steps.instruction: Look at the word: rabbit. Mark the vowels.
steps.explanation: Every syllable has one vowel sound. Since this word has two vowels, it likely has two syllables.

steps.instruction: Look at the consonants between the two vowels. When there are two consonants between vowels (VC/CV), split between them.
steps.work: rab | bit (split between the two b's)
steps.explanation: There are two b's between the vowels 'a' and 'i'. The VC/CV rule says to split between those consonants.

steps.instruction: Read each syllable, then put them together.
steps.explanation: Each syllable is a closed syllable (ends in a consonant), so the vowels are short. Read each part, then blend them: rabbit!

title: Splitting a V/CV Word into Syllables

steps.instruction: Look at the word: robot. Mark the vowels.
steps.work: r-o-b-o-t → vowels are the first 'o' and the second 'o'
steps.explanation: This word has two vowel sounds, so it has two syllables.

steps.instruction: There is only one consonant ('b') between the vowels. Try the V/CV split first: split before the consonant.
steps.explanation: When there is one consonant between vowels, first try splitting before it (V/CV). This makes the first syllable open.

steps.instruction: An open syllable (ending in a vowel) usually has a long vowel sound. Does 'ro' sound right with a long 'o'?
steps.explanation: The first syllable 'ro' ends in a vowel, so the 'o' says its name: /roh/. This sounds correct.
The word is 'robot'. The V/CV pattern often gives the first syllable a long vowel sound. If it doesn't sound right, try splitting after the consonant instead (VC/V).
